title: CSRF removed for test convenience
description: State-changing browser-authenticated routes disable CSRF or origin checks to make tests pass.
body: Failure mode: integration friction leads to disabling CSRF middleware, same-site checks, or origin validation in a code path that can reach production. Tests go green while browser-based cross-site attacks become easier. Detection signals include disabled middleware, broad route exemptions, mutation routes missing token checks, and no negative cross-origin test. Remediation is fixing test setup or using scoped local-only bypasses that cannot ship. Human gate is required for any production exemption. Use when a proposed approach skips trust boundaries, authorization decisions, dangerous sinks, secrets, and dependency execution or claims success without domain evidence. Do not use this dossier as a positive implementation strategy; it is a do-not-use signal. Actions: block the shortcut, name the missing evidence, and switch to a safer methodology or ask for human approval. Validation signals include absence or contradiction in negative security tests, server-side checks, sink payload tests, and secret scans. Risk boundary: treat repeated recurrence as a blocker when it can cause client-only controls, injection, credential leakage, permissive fallback, and unsafe dependency changes.
